steps.text: Assess the suitability of potential collaborations with not-for-profit and for-profit organizations.
Apply guiding principles to ensure scientific meetings are conducted in a meritorious and unbiased manner.
Obtain approval from the director of the CC/CO/NC(s), or his/her designee, for requests for CDC sponsorship of conferences that include funding.
Process the funded request through financial support procedures as described in Section 4H (1) or (2).
Obtain approval from the director of the CC/CO/NC(s), or his/her designee, for requests for CDC sponsorship of conferences that do not include funding.
Clear all promotional or publicity materials for the event with CDC (applicable to non-federal sponsors).
Approve or disapprove requests for technical assistance, participation in, or attending a conference by CDC staff members according to procedures determined by the employee's CC/CO/NC.

Provide CDC with documentation of a plan with a timeline for substantive involvement in, and development of, the content of the meeting, including standards that ensure scientific and public health merit.
Provide a written process of how presentations or abstracts are to be reviewed and criteria that will be used for selecting presentations.
Agree to abide by CDC Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences, when applicable.
Establish a written process for approving conference funding, including solicitation info, proposal elements, review/approval process, and documentation process.
Follow financial support procedures as specified by the funding CC/CO/NC, Procurement and Grants Office (PGO), and the Financial Management Office (FMO).
Determine the appropriate funding instrument (acquisition award for federal conference, assistance award for non-federal conference).
Identify the conference prior to the award if supported by assistance awards other than Conference Grants.
Obtain prior approval from the Grants Management Officer if the conference is proposed post-award.
Include the specified language (Reference 7D) in all program announcements, including conference support announcements, for CDC sponsorship of nonfederal conferences when funds are to be awarded.
Submit a formal request in writing to the CCs/COs/NCs having related program.
Assure that the proposed meeting facilities comply with ADAAG prior to approving any request or issuing any obligation documents.
Obtain written certification of ADA compliance from facility management, or ensure the CC/CO/NC completes the Accessible Meetings Checklist and keeps it on file.
Maintain a copy of all written correspondence regarding sponsored conferences on file in the Office of the Director.
Ensure agreements and records account fully and accurately for each party's programmatic and financial responsibilities.
Describe separately in agreements and records any discrete portion of an event exclusively sponsored by CDC or a non-Federal entity.
Make agreements and records concerning the amounts, sources, and uses of funds available to the public.
Assign a specific person responsibility for maintaining records and submitting a quarterly line listing of all conferences sponsored or disapproved to the OD, CDC.
Submit a quarterly line listing of all conferences sponsored or disapproved to the OD, CDC (Deputy Chief Operating Officer).
OD maintains records of these listings.
OD makes listings available to the CC/CO/NCs.
Clarify how the potential collaboration fits within CDC's overall mission and priorities and the private partner's mission and priorities.
Address independence and objectivity of scientific judgment in the potential collaboration.
Identify mechanisms that will ensure objectivity and independence during the collaboration.
Assess the effect of the private partner's products or services, if applicable, on health and whether they are compatible with CDC's mission.

Assess the behavior of the private partner in conducting business and determine whether the partner's behavior is consistent with CDC's mission and the principles guiding private sector collaboration.
Assess the specific area of mutual interest and the public impact of the partner's broad public mission and image.
Seek multiple collaborators representing a broad spectrum of opinions and interests.
Inform private partners that the existence of the collaborative relationship will be made a matter of public record.
Work out early how the public record disclosure will occur.
Establish a formal review and advisory process within each coordinating center (CC), coordinating office (CO), or national center (NC) for examining potential collaborations.
Ensure employees developing collaborations with the private sector receive orientation and guidance from the CDC Ethics Officer.

preconditions.text: The guiding principles are applicable to the conference format and purpose.
The conference must support CDC's mission and further CDC's mission of promoting health and quality of life by preventing and controlling disease, injury, and disability.
Scientific meetings sponsored by CDC must meet the Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences outlined in Section 3 of this policy.
Sponsorship must be consistent with CDC's policy on Guidance for Collaboration with the Private Sector and the HHS Office of the General Counsel (OGC), Ethics Division Co-Sponsorship Guidance.
Prior to receiving funding (for an external conference organizer)
For conferences/meetings funded directly by the CCs/COs/NCs
A grant, cooperative agreement, interagency agreement, contract, or purchase order is required when CDC is asked to sponsor a conference with a nonfederal organization and funds are provided.
A co-sponsorship agreement is necessary whenever CDC is asked to sponsor a conference with a non-federal organization, regardless of whether funds are provided.
The support must have approval by the Director, or his/her designee, of the CC/CO/NC(s) providing the funds.
CDC grantees using general program funds to support conferences must have CDC approval if CDC is to be identified as a sponsor.
The conference must be within the authorized programmatic scope and integral to successful completion of the specific project.
Sponsorship of a conference is requested and does not involve expenditure of CDC funds.
ADA compliance for the meeting facility has been previously assured, documentation is maintained, and the facility has not undergone renovation.
If dealing with highly controversial issues

constraints.text: Public health decisions must be based on sound science and the public good.
The benefits to society must be a higher goal than benefits to either partner in the collaboration.
The agency must be a diligent steward of public trust and funds.
The agency and its employees should conduct business according to the ethical standards that govern the agency.
Scientific meetings and conferences should be unbiased forums for exchange of scientific information and consider the implications for public health.
Scientific meetings and conferences should provide venues for open scientific discourse.
Organizers of scientific meetings and conferences should provide a transparent process for selecting presentations based primarily on scientific merit.
Any conference sponsored by CDC that is held in domestic facilities must be fully accessible to the public as required by the Americans with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG).
The conference organizer(s) may use CDC's name only in factual publicity for the conference.
CDC sponsored conferences will be guided by sound scientific and programmatic oversight.
Organizers of scientific conferences will adhere to CDC Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences.
The organizer is responsible for ensuring that CDC-sponsored conferences/meetings are managed in a transparent, objective, and unbiased manner.

The review process must include at minimum review by a Center or Division level appointed committee composed of 3 staff members or peers per meeting, and final approval must be made by the CC/CO/NC Director.
Contingency awards will be made allowing usage of only 10% of the total amount to be awarded until a final full agenda is approved by CDC.
All scientific conferences receiving CDC funds must meet CDC’s Guiding Principles for the Conduct of Scientific Conferences.

Written certification or documentation must address: adequate accessible parking, accessible entrance/doors/hardware, wheelchair maneuvering space, accessible water/toilets, fixed seating/tables, sales/service counters, and auxiliary aids.
A public accommodation must provide auxiliary aids and services (e.g., interpreters, headsets, captioning, TDDs, Braille) when necessary to ensure effective communication.
Records must include documentation required as per Section 4H.
CDC shall not co-sponsor an event with an entity that will not make information concerning funding publicly available.
CDC should avoid participating in indirect collaborations unless it would participate in a direct relationship with the partners.
No direct endorsement of a product or service is permitted by the agency.
Maintain a process of openness and fairness in developing collaborations with partners on similar projects or projects with similar intent.
